title_slide: The Lie We Inherited About Strength

slides.heading: The Lie We Inherited About Strength
bullet_points: Break a bone — people sign your cast.
Admit you're drowning — and the room goes quiet.
We were taught the wrong definition of strength.
slides.speaker_note: Open with the core contrast to establish what we're challenging — the uneven social permission around physical versus emotional pain.

slides.heading: The numbers don't lie
bullet_points: 40% of men won't talk about their mental health to anyone.
Globally, men die by suicide at significantly higher rates than women.
This isn't weakness — it's a systemic failure to give people permission to struggle out loud.
slides.speaker_note: Ground the emotional argument in data before going deeper into the personal — anchors the stakes.

slides.heading: The stigma is inherited, not chosen
bullet_points: 'Real men don't cry.'
'Therapy is for people who can't handle life.'
These scripts ran so long they became wallpaper — nobody notices them anymore, but they're still running.
slides.speaker_note: Name the mechanism — inherited scripts — so the audience can identify it in their own lives rather than feeling blamed.

slides.heading: We confuse support with problem-solving
bullet_points: Someone calls because they need to be heard.
We reach for budgeting tools, action plans, and logic.
Some moments don't need a mechanic — they need a witness.
slides.speaker_note: Use the late-night phone call story to make the pattern visceral and relatable before offering the reframe.

slides.heading: The personal trainer double standard
bullet_points: No stigma: hiring a personal trainer to get physically stronger.
Automatic stigma: hiring a therapist to get emotionally stronger.
Same logic. Completely different social response.
slides.speaker_note: This analogy lands hard — let it sit. Don't rush past it.

slides.heading: Two kinds of strength
bullet_points: Strength built on suppression looks solid — until the right pressure hits.
Strength built on honesty bends — but doesn't break.
Vulnerability isn't the opposite of strength. It's proof of it.
slides.speaker_note: This is the emotional peak of the deck — let the reframe breathe before moving on.

slides.heading: What breaking silence actually looks like
bullet_points: Ask how someone is — and actually wait for the real answer.
Listen without immediately pivoting to solutions.
Mention therapy without treating it like a last resort or a verdict.
slides.speaker_note: Keep this concrete and small — not campaigns or grand gestures, but things anyone can do today.

slides.heading: The wall cracks one conversation at a time
bullet_points: You don't have to carry your struggle alone.
Neither does the person sitting next to you.
Start there.
slides.speaker_note: Close with permission and invitation — not a call to heroism but a call to small, honest action.
